explicit_text: I was raised in a traditional family in a village in Japan, where life for a wife was very difficult. Since I was 14 or 15, I wanted to come to America because I didn't want to live in such a poverty-stricken Japan. When we visited Japan last time, we kept our visit quiet to avoid big celebrations, and my brothers who worked at universities were surprised to see us. My mother had great foresight about life in Japan and encouraged me to go to America for a better future.
implicit_text: I was raised in a traditional family in a village in a country in East Asia, where life for a wife was very difficult. Since I was 14 or 15, I wanted to come to America because I didn't want to live in such a poverty-stricken place. When we visited my homeland last time, we kept our visit quiet to avoid big celebrations, and my brothers who worked at universities were surprised to see us. My mother had great foresight about life there and encouraged me to go to America for a better future.

explicit_text: I came to the United States because I wanted a better life than the one I had in Japan. My husband had just graduated from the University of California, Davis, and though he didn't have much money, he was able to support us. We lived in places like Isleton and Florin, where our children attended segregated schools. Despite the hardships, I saw the patriotic spirit of Nisei Americans, especially during the war, and I felt that America offered opportunities for our children that Japan could not.
implicit_text: I came to this country because I wanted a better life than the one I had in my homeland. My husband had just graduated from a university in California, and though he didn't have much money, he was able to support us. We lived in places like Isleton and Florin, where our children attended segregated schools. Despite the hardships, I saw the patriotic spirit of second-generation Japanese Americans, especially during the war, and I felt that this country offered opportunities for our children that my homeland could not.

explicit_text: My husband, Mr. Itano, graduated from the University of California, Davis, with a degree in Agriculture. At that time, he didn't have much money, but my mother believed he would be able to support me, and she was right. His education at U.C. Davis was the foundation for our life in America, even though it was a modest start. This connection to U.C. Davis was important in our early years here.

His education at that university was the foundation for our life in America, even though it was a modest start. This connection to that California university was important in our early years here.

explicit_text: We used to live in Isleton, California, where our children attended segregated schools. The Japanese community there was close-knit, but the schools were separate, and many Nisei children had heavy accents because of it. People in Sacramento wondered if all young people in Isleton were Kibei because of their Japanese language skills. Isleton was part of our early American life and shaped our children's experiences with education and community.
implicit_text: We used to live in a small town in California called Isleton, where our children attended segregated schools. The Japanese community there was close-knit, but the schools were separate, and many second-generation children had heavy accents because of it. People in Sacramento wondered if all young people in Isleton were Kibei because of their Japanese language skills. Isleton was part of our early American life and shaped our children's experiences with education and community.

explicit_text: Florin was another place where Japanese children, including my own, went to segregated schools. I heard stories about Nisei from Florin who spoke Japanese with heavy accents but struggled with English. It was a common experience for many Japanese-American families in Florin, reflecting the challenges of assimilation and education during those times. Florin was part of the broader Japanese-American community in California where we lived.

explicit_text: When I visited Livingston, I heard about a Nisei resident from the Florin area who had difficulty speaking English. My son attended a public school there where teachers would ask simple questions, and he sometimes answered in Japanese because his English was not strong yet. Although it was not a segregated school, there were many Japanese and Chinese children, and few Caucasians. Livingston was part of the educational journey for our children in America.

explicit_text: My children attended Lincoln Elementary School, where the principal was a very strict woman. If boys misbehaved, teachers would warn them about being sent to the principal, and that was enough to make them behave. The school had many Japanese and Chinese children, and it was a place where discipline and education were taken seriously. Lincoln Elementary was an important part of our children's early schooling experience.

explicit_text: People in Sacramento were curious about the Japanese youth from Isleton, wondering if they were all Kibei because of their Japanese language skills. When we entered the concentration camp, there was talk in Sacramento about the language abilities of Nisei children. Sacramento was a community where many Japanese families lived and where the struggles and achievements of Japanese Americans were observed. It was part of the broader context of our lives before and during the war.

explicit_text: My daughter attended Seishin Jogakuin, a women's college in Japan, after graduating from high school. She went there just before the war started, but the conflict interrupted her education. Seishin Jogakuin was part of her formative years and represented a connection to Japan even as our family was living in America. It was a significant institution for her education during a turbulent time.

explicit_text: I knew a Nisei who graduated from Harvard University, but despite his education, the only job he could find was as a delivery boy. This showed the discrimination and challenges Japanese Americans faced even with prestigious degrees. Harvard represented the high level of education some Nisei achieved, but also the harsh reality of limited opportunities during those times. It was a bittersweet example of the struggles for recognition and employment.
implicit_text: I knew a second-generation Japanese American who graduated from an Ivy League university in Massachusetts, but despite his education, the only job he could find was as a delivery boy. This showed the discrimination and challenges Japanese Americans faced even with prestigious degrees. That university represented the high level of education some second-generation Japanese Americans achieved, but also the harsh reality of limited opportunities during those times. It was a bittersweet example of the struggles for recognition and employment.

explicit_text: The soldiers of the 442nd Infantry Regiment were Nisei who showed maximum effort and patriotism during the war. They were motivated by the phrase 'Makeruna'—'Don't get defeated'—which they heard from their parents. Their dedication was not just about loving their own country but also about cooperating with the American government and proving their loyalty through actions. The 442nd represented the courage and spirit of Japanese Americans during a difficult time.

Their dedication was not just about loving their own country but also about cooperating with the American government and proving their loyalty through actions. That regiment represented the courage and spirit of Japanese Americans during a difficult time.

explicit_text: I was at Tule Lake during the war, and it was a difficult time for our family. While I was there, my daughter Masako was in Minnesota, and we had to decide whether to return to Japan or stay in America. Masako wrote to me saying she wanted to stay and serve America, even if she was alone. Tule Lake was a place of hardship but also a moment when we saw the strength and patriotism of our family and community.

That relocation center was a place of hardship but also a moment when we saw the strength and patriotism of our family and community.

explicit_text: My son Dean is a lawyer and also the manager of Guild Savings and Loan. This was a proud achievement for our family, showing how far we had come in America. Guild Savings and Loan was part of his professional life and a symbol of success after many struggles. It represented the opportunities that opened up for our children despite earlier hardships.

explicit_text: My son Masashi works as a pathologist at Long Beach Memorial Medical Center. This is a great accomplishment for him and for our family, reflecting the professional paths our children took. Long Beach Memorial Medical Center is where he applies his medical expertise, and it shows the progress we made in America. It is a source of pride that our children have such respected careers.

explicit_text: My son went to college in Minnesota, originally wanting to become a teacher but later becoming an engineer. Also, one of Mrs. Hiraga's sons went to college in Minnesota and later became a minister turned social worker. Minnesota was a place where some of our children pursued higher education and began their careers. It was part of the broader American landscape where Japanese Americans sought opportunities.

explicit_text: My son Dan was attending a dental college in Chicago when my husband died. Also, Helen, another child, learned sewing in Chicago after evacuation disrupted her junior college education. Chicago was a city connected to our family's educational and professional experiences during and after the war. It was a place of transition and growth for us.

explicit_text: I have been reading 'Purine Tomo,' a magazine for housewives, which often quoted from the Bible. One writer, Hanis Motokota, said she got ideas for her school from the Bible, especially about teaching children not to be defeated but to do their best. The Bible's teachings influenced how some in our community thought about perseverance and kindness. It was a source of wisdom and encouragement during challenging times.

explicit_text: I have been reading 'Purine Tomo,' a magazine for housewives, for a long time. It contained many good articles, including those by Hanis Motokota, who often quoted the Bible. The magazine provided valuable advice and inspiration, especially about teaching children to do their best and be kind whether they win or lose. 'Purine Tomo' was an important part of my life and helped me understand how to encourage my children.
